title: Interview Templates
description: Drill the most common algorithmic templates that appear in coding interviews. Each pattern provides a reusable C++ skeleton you can adapt to dozens of problems, so recognizing and applying them becomes second nature.

sections.title: Sliding window
sections.explanation: The sliding window maintains a fixed- or variable-size window over a sequence, updating the result incrementally. Instead of recalculating from scratch each time, subtract the element leaving the window and add the one entering. This turns O(n*k) brute force into O(n), which interviewers expect you to recognize immediately.

exercise.prompt: Complete the sliding window function to find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k. Print the result for nums = {2, 3, 1, 5, 4, 2} with k = 3.

validation.message: Maximum sum subarray of size 3 is {1,5,4}+{5,4,2}? Actually {3,1,5}=9, {1,5,4}=10, {5,4,2}=11 — answer is 11
Subtract the leaving element with nums[i-k] and track best with max()

exercise.hint: Subtract nums[i - k] (the element sliding out). Use max(best, window) to track the running maximum.

sections.title: Binary search
sections.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration. The template uses two pointers — `left` and `right` — with a `while (left <= right)` loop. Compute `mid = left + (right - left) / 2` to avoid overflow. Interviewers test edge cases like off-by-one errors, so drill this until it's automatic.

exercise.prompt: Complete the binary search function. Find the index of target 10 in {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12}. Print the result.

validation.message: 10 is at index 4 in the sorted array
Use <= in the loop condition, and move left/right by mid +/- 1

exercise.hint: Loop while left <= right. Move left = mid + 1 when too small, right = mid - 1 when too large.

sections.title: Two pointers
sections.explanation: The two-pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end of a sorted array, moving them inward based on a condition. For pair-sum: if the sum is too small, advance left; too large, retreat right. This solves in O(n) time with O(1) space — a tradeoff interviewers love to discuss.

exercise.prompt: Complete the two-pointer function to find indices of two numbers that sum to target. Test with nums = {1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 11} and target = 10. Print the two indices.

validation.message: Increment left by 1 when sum too small, decrement right by 1 when too large

exercise.hint: When sum < target, increment left by 1. When sum > target, decrement right by 1.

sections.title: Prefix sum
sections.explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums so that any range-sum query becomes O(1). Build it as `prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]`, then `rangeSum(l, r) = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]`. Building is O(n), each query is O(1). This pattern underlies subarray-sum problems and serves as a building block for more complex solutions.

exercise.prompt: Build a prefix sum array for {2, 5, 1, 7, 3}. Print the sum of elements from index 1 to 3 (inclusive), which should be 5+1+7 = 13.

validation.message: Build with prefix[i] and query with prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]

exercise.hint: prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]. Range sum from l to r = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l].

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores all candidate solutions with the **choose-explore-unchoose** pattern. For each candidate, push it to the path, recurse deeper, then pop it and try the next. This template drives permutation, combination, and constraint-satisfaction problems. Prune branches early to stay tractable.

exercise.prompt: Generate all permutations of {1, 2, 3} using backtracking. Print the total count of permutations.

validation.message: Unchoose with pop_back() and reset used[i] = false after recursion

exercise.hint: After the recursive call, unchoose: path.pop_back() and used[i] = false.
